terms.meaning: Four graph nodes; each maps to one row and one column
A[i][i]=0 for a simple graph with no self-loops
terms.example: A[3][3]=0 since P3 is not its own neighbour

given.aim: Construct the 4x4 adjacency matrix for the given undirected graph.
given.plan: For each edge (Pi,Pj) set A[i][j]=A[j][i]=1; all other entries including the diagonal remain 0.

steps.note: Undirected graph so each edge produces two symmetric entries.
steps.title: Identify edges from the figure

steps.note: Symmetric matrix confirms undirected graph; zero diagonal confirms simple graph.
steps.title: Fill the adjacency matrix

questions.question: Write the adjacency matrix representation of the graph given in the figure. The graph has four vertices P1, P2, P3, P4 with edges P1-P2, P1-P3, P2-P4, and P3-P4 (a 4-cycle).

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 300 200' width='300' height='200'><style>.hi{fill:#CECBF6;stroke:#7F77DD;stroke-width:3;}.ink{fill:#2C2C2A;}.lbl{font-size:16px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,sans-serif;}</style><circle class='hi' cx='75' cy='55' r='22'/><text class='lbl ink' x='75' y='61' text-anchor='middle'>P1</text><circle class='hi' cx='225' cy='55' r='22'/><text class='lbl ink' x='225' y='61' text-anchor='middle'>P2</text><circle class='hi' cx='75' cy='155' r='22'/><text class='lbl ink' x='75' y='161' text-anchor='middle'>P3</text><circle class='hi' cx='225' cy='155' r='22'/><text class='lbl ink' x='225' y='161' text-anchor='middle'>P4</text><line x1='97' y1='55' x2='203' y2='55' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='75' y1='77' x2='75' y2='133' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='203' y1='67' x2='97' y2='143' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='225' y1='77' x2='225' y2='133' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/></svg>

keywords.explain: An n x n matrix where A[i][j]=1 iff vertices i and j share an edge

keywords.example: Edge P1-P3 gives A[1][3]=1 and A[3][1]=1
keywords.explain: For undirected graphs A[i][j]=A[j][i]; each edge gives two symmetric 1-entries

understand.visual_alt: P1 (top-left) connected to P2 (top-right) and P3 (bottom-left); P2 connected to P4 (bottom-right) via a cross-edge; P3 connected to P4.
understand.plain: An adjacency matrix is a square matrix where A[i][j]=1 if vertices i and j share an edge, 0 otherwise. For undirected graphs the matrix is symmetric. You scan all edges and place 1s in the corresponding row-column positions.

questions.formula_note: The adjacency matrix definition A[i][j]=1 iff edge (Pi,Pj) exists is the core tool; symmetry of the result confirms the graph is undirected.
questions.to_find: Adjacency matrix of the given graph

terms.meaning: Number of vertices
terms.example: n=4: both G and G-bar can have 3 edges

terms.meaning: Total edges in K_n shared between G and G-bar
terms.connects: Must be even for equal split

terms.meaning: Key divisibility condition for equal split to be an integer
terms.example: n=0 mod 4 or n=1 mod 4 satisfy this

given.aim: Find all residues r mod 4 such that edges(G) = edges(G-bar) is possible.
given.plan: Set edges(G) = n(n-1)/4; require this to be an integer so 4 | n(n-1); check each residue class mod 4.

steps.apply: edges(G)=edges(G-bar) requires 2*edges(G)=C(n,2)=n(n-1)/2, so edges(G)=n(n-1)/4 must be a non-negative integer. This requires 4 | n(n-1).
steps.note: n and n-1 are consecutive so their product is always even, but divisibility by 4 is the tighter condition.
steps.title: Derive the equal-split condition

steps.apply: n=0 mod 4: n=4k => 4k*(4k-1) divisible by 4. Valid. n=1 mod 4: n-1=4k => (4k+1)*4k divisible by 4. Valid. n=2 mod 4: n(n-1)=(4k+2)(4k+1)=2*(2k+1)(4k+1); only one factor of 2, not div by 4. Invalid. n=3 mod 4: n(n-1)=(4k+3)(4k+2)=2*(4k+3)(2k+1); only one factor of 2. Invalid.
steps.note: Only residues 0 and 1 mod 4 give a valid integer edge count.
steps.title: Check all residues mod 4

solution.result: n must be congruent to 0 or 1 modulo 4.

questions.answer: 0 or 1 (n is congruent to 0 mod 4 or 1 mod 4)
questions.question: A graph which has the same number of edges as its complement must have number of vertices congruent to ________ or ________ modulo 4.

keywords.example: n=4: C(4,2)=6; G and G-bar each need 3 edges for equal split
keywords.explain: G-bar has the same vertices as G but contains exactly the edges NOT in G
4 | n(n-1) is required for the equal-split edge count to be an integer

understand.visual_alt: G and G-bar partition edges of K_n; equal partition requires n(n-1)/4 to be an integer, forcing n mod 4 to be 0 or 1.
understand.plain: G and its complement G-bar partition all C(n,2) edges of K_n. Equal edge counts require C(n,2) to be even, i.e., n(n-1)/4 must be an integer. Since n and n-1 are consecutive, 4 divides n(n-1) exactly when n = 0 mod 4 (so 4 divides n) or n = 1 mod 4 (so 4 divides n-1).

questions.formula_note: The condition n(n-1)/4 being an integer is central; checking which residues mod 4 make 4 | n(n-1) yields n = 0 or 1 mod 4.
questions.to_find: Residues mod 4 such that a graph and its complement have equal edge counts

terms.meaning: Total vertices in the graph
Number of connected components
terms.example: k=2 forces at least 2 vertices, one per component
terms.connects: Each extra component beyond one costs one clique vertex

terms.meaning: Edges in the optimal large complete component

given.aim: Maximize the edge count in a graph with exactly k components and n total vertices.
given.plan: Use k-1 isolated vertices for k-1 components and form K_{n-k+1} with the remaining vertices. Edges = C(n-k+1,2).

steps.apply: Optimal: k-1 isolated-vertex components (0 edges each), one component with n-k+1 vertices as a complete graph. Splitting vertices between components always loses edges since C(a+b,2) > C(a,2)+C(b,2) for a,b >= 1.
steps.note: Consolidating into one large clique is always better.
steps.title: Allocate vertices optimally

steps.apply: m = n-k+1 vertices: edges = C(n-k+1,2) = (n-k+1)(n-k)/2. Example: n=6, k=2 => C(5,2)=10 edges.
steps.note: When k=1, max edges = C(n,2), the complete graph K_n.
steps.title: Count edges in the large clique

questions.question: The maximum number of possible edges in an undirected graph with n vertices and k components is ______.

keywords.example: K_4 plus one isolated vertex has 2 components and C(4,2)=6 edges
keywords.explain: A maximal connected subgraph; k components means the graph has k separate pieces
K_m has every possible edge among m vertices, giving C(m,2) edges

understand.visual_alt: One large clique K_{n-k+1} plus k-1 isolated vertices: the optimal configuration giving C(n-k+1,2) maximum edges.
understand.plain: To maximize edges while keeping exactly k components, put as many vertices as possible into one component (making it a complete subgraph) and use k-1 isolated vertices for the other components. One component gets n-k+1 vertices, giving C(n-k+1,2) edges.

questions.formula_note: Concentrating all non-isolated vertices into one clique is optimal; the max-edges-k-components formula C(n-k+1,2) gives the maximum edge count directly.
questions.to_find: Maximum edge count given n vertices and k components

terms.meaning: Total vertices across all p components
Number of tree components in the forest
terms.connects: Subtracted once from n in the formula

terms.meaning: Total edge count
terms.connects: Direct closed-form answer from tree edge formula

given.aim: Count total edges in a forest given total vertex count n and component count p.
given.plan: Each tree with n_i vertices contributes n_i-1 edges. Sum: total edges = (sum n_i) - p = n - p.

steps.apply: Component i has n_i vertices => n_i-1 edges. Total = sum_{i=1}^{p}(n_i-1) = n - p.
steps.note: Works because a forest is exactly a disjoint union of trees.
steps.title: Apply tree edge formula per component
steps.formula_raw: a tree on m vertices has exactly m-1 edges

steps.apply: Direct: edges = n - p. Example: n=8, p=3 => 5 edges. Three trees of sizes 4,2,2 have 3+1+1=5 edges. Correct.
steps.note: p=1 gives n-1 edges, consistent with the single-tree case.
steps.title: Verify with the forest formula
steps.formula_raw: a forest with n vertices and p tree-components has n - p edges

solution.result: A forest with n vertices and p components has exactly n - p edges.

questions.question: How many edges can there be in a forest with p components having n vertices in all?

keywords.example: Two disjoint paths P3 form a forest with 4 vertices, 2 components, 2=4-2 edges
keywords.explain: An acyclic undirected graph; every connected component is a tree

keywords.example: A star K_{1,4} has 5 vertices and 4=5-1 edges
keywords.explain: A tree on m vertices has exactly m-1 edges

understand.visual_alt: A forest of p trees; tree i with n_i vertices contributes n_i-1 edges, totaling n-p edges over all components.
understand.plain: A forest is an acyclic graph; each of its p connected components is a tree. A tree on m vertices has exactly m-1 edges. Summing over all p components: total edges = (n_1-1)+(n_2-1)+...+(n_p-1) = n - p.

questions.formula_note: The tree edge formula m-1 applied per component and summed over p components directly yields the forest formula edges = n - p.
questions.to_find: Number of edges in a forest with p components and n total vertices

terms.meaning: Two extra edges beyond a spanning tree (n-1 edges)

terms.example: G has 2 fundamental cycles relative to any spanning tree
terms.connects: Proves C true; governs edge-removal connectivity

terms.meaning: An edge whose removal disconnects the graph; lies on no cycle
terms.example: With cycle rank 2, specific edge pairs can disconnect G
terms.connects: Governs truth of B and D

given.aim: Determine which of the five statements are true for G with n vertices and n+1 edges.
given.plan: Compute cycle rank = 2; conclude G has cycles (C true); analyze connectivity after removing 1 and 2 edges.

steps.apply: Cycle rank = (n+1)-n+1 = 2. G has 2 independent cycles. A (no cycles) is FALSE; C (at least one cycle) is TRUE.
steps.note: Cycle rank > 0 is sufficient to confirm cycles exist.
steps.title: Compute cycle rank of G
steps.formula_raw: number of independent cycles = E - V + (number of components)

steps.apply: B: removing any single cycle edge does not disconnect G (the rest of that cycle provides an alternate path). B is FALSE. D: since cycle rank = 2, there exist two edges (one per independent cycle) whose simultaneous removal eliminates all alternate paths and disconnects G. D is TRUE.
steps.note: D says there EXIST two edges that disconnect G, not that every pair of edges disconnects G.
steps.title: Evaluate statements B and D
steps.formula_raw: an edge is a bridge iff it does not lie on any cycle

solution.result: C and D are true. G has at least one cycle (cycle rank 2) and there exist two edges whose removal disconnects it.

questions.question:
Consider a simple connected graph G with n vertices and n+1 edges (n > 2). Then, which of the following statements are true?
A. G has no cycles
B. The graph obtained by removing any edge from G is not connected
C. G has at least one cycle
D. The graph obtained by removing any two edges from G is not connected
E. None of the above

keywords.example: n vertices, n+1 edges, 1 component: cycle rank = 2
keywords.explain: E-V+components; measures edges above a spanning tree

keywords.example: A graph with 2 vertex-disjoint triangles has cycle rank 2
keywords.explain: Two cycles are independent if neither is a subset of the other; cycle rank = 2 means 2 independent cycles

understand.visual_alt: G with n vertices and n+1 edges has cycle rank 2: at least one cycle exists (C true) and removing 2 appropriate edges disconnects G (D true).
understand.plain: A spanning tree uses n-1 edges; G has n+1 edges, which is 2 more. Cycle rank = E-V+1 = (n+1)-n+1 = 2. G has 2 independent cycles. So G definitely contains a cycle (C true, A false). Removing one cycle edge keeps G connected via the rest of that cycle (B false). But removing 2 edges, one from each independent cycle, can disconnect G (D true).

questions.formula_note: Cycle rank E-V+1=2 is the key: it confirms cycles exist (C true) and that two independent cycle edges can be removed to disconnect G (D true).
questions.to_find: Which statements hold for a connected graph G with n vertices and n+1 edges

terms.meaning: Only the single isolated node exists
Edge subsets of the one possible edge: empty or present
0 edges: empty; 1 edge: edge+isolated; 2 edges: path P3; 3 edges: K3

given.aim: Count all structurally distinct (non-isomorphic) simple graphs on at most 3 vertices.

steps.apply: n=1: 1 graph. n=2: C(2,2)=1 possible edge, 2 subsets => 2 non-isomorphic graphs. Subtotal = 3.
steps.note: Two 2-vertex graphs differ in edge count so they are non-isomorphic.
steps.title: Count for n=1 and n=2

steps.apply: C(3,2)=3 possible edges. By edge count: 0 edges (1 class: empty), 1 edge (1 class: edge+isolated), 2 edges (1 class: path P3), 3 edges (1 class: K3). Total = 4.
steps.note: For n=3 each possible edge count yields exactly one isomorphism class.
steps.title: Count non-isomorphic graphs for n=3

steps.note: Non-isomorphic count grows rapidly with n but stays small for n <= 3.
steps.title: Sum all counts

solution.result: 7 distinct non-isomorphic simple graphs with up to 3 nodes.

questions.question:
The number of distinct simple graphs with up to three nodes is:
A. 15
B. 10
C. 7
D. 9

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 420 100' width='420' height='100'><style>.hi{fill:#CECBF6;stroke:#7F77DD;stroke-width:2;}.ink{fill:#2C2C2A;}.lbl{font-size:12px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,sans-serif;}</style><text class='lbl ink' x='5' y='15'>n=1</text><circle class='hi' cx='25' cy='55' r='12'/><text class='lbl ink' x='55' y='15'>n=2</text><circle class='hi' cx='70' cy='40' r='10'/><circle class='hi' cx='70' cy='70' r='10'/><circle class='hi' cx='105' cy='40' r='10'/><circle class='hi' cx='105' cy='70' r='10'/><line x1='105' y1='50' x2='105' y2='60' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text class='lbl ink' x='135' y='15'>n=3: 4 graphs</text><circle class='hi' cx='155' cy='40' r='9'/><circle class='hi' cx='175' cy='40' r='9'/><circle class='hi' cx='165' cy='65' r='9'/><circle class='hi' cx='205' cy='40' r='9'/><circle class='hi' cx='225' cy='40' r='9'/><circle class='hi' cx='215' cy='65' r='9'/><line x1='205' y1='40' x2='225' y2='40' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><circle class='hi' cx='255' cy='40' r='9'/><circle class='hi' cx='275' cy='40' r='9'/><circle class='hi' cx='265' cy='65' r='9'/><line x1='255' y1='40' x2='275' y2='40' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='255' y1='49' x2='265' y2='56' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><circle class='hi' cx='305' cy='40' r='9'/><circle class='hi' cx='325' cy='40' r='9'/><circle class='hi' cx='315' cy='65' r='9'/><line x1='305' y1='40' x2='325' y2='40' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='305' y1='49' x2='315' y2='56' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='325' y1='49' x2='315' y2='56' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/></svg>

keywords.example: For n=3: path P3 and triangle K3 are non-isomorphic
keywords.explain: Structurally distinct graphs that cannot be made identical by relabeling vertices

keywords.example: K_3 is the unique simple 3-vertex graph with all 3 edges
keywords.explain: Undirected, no self-loops, at most one edge per pair of vertices

understand.visual_alt: Seven non-isomorphic simple graphs: 1 with 1 node, 2 with 2 nodes, and 4 with 3 nodes (by edge count 0 through 3).
understand.plain: Count non-isomorphic simple graphs on 1, 2, and 3 vertices separately. n=1: 1 graph (isolated node). n=2: 2 graphs (no edge or one edge). n=3: 4 graphs (by edge count 0, 1, 2, or 3 edges; each count gives exactly one isomorphism class). Total = 1+2+4 = 7.

questions.formula_note: No single formula applies; the count comes from exhaustive enumeration per vertex count, grouping by edge count (one isomorphism class per edge count for n <= 3).
questions.to_find: Count of non-isomorphic simple graphs on 1, 2, or 3 vertices

terms.meaning: The common degree of every vertex
terms.example: d=3: every vertex has exactly 3 neighbours
terms.connects: Multiplied by n gives the total degree sum nd

terms.meaning: Total number of vertices in the graph
Total edge count; must be integer so nd must be even

given.aim: Derive the number of edges in a d-regular graph on n vertices.
given.plan: Apply the handshaking lemma: sum of degrees = nd = 2|E|. Solve for |E| = nd/2.

steps.apply: Each of n vertices has degree d. Total degree sum = n * d. By the handshaking lemma: nd = 2|E|.
steps.note: nd must be even for |E| to be an integer; this constrains which (n,d) pairs form valid regular graphs.
steps.title: Compute total degree sum
steps.formula_raw: sum of deg(v) over all v = 2|E|

steps.apply: |E| = nd/2. Verification: Petersen graph (n=10, d=3): |E| = 30/2 = 15. K_4 (n=4, d=3): |E| = 12/2 = 6 = C(4,2). Both confirmed.
steps.note: Formula applies to any d-regular graph regardless of its specific structure.
steps.title: Solve for the number of edges
steps.formula_raw: a d-regular graph on n vertices has nd/2 edges

solution.result: The number of edges in a d-regular graph on n vertices is nd/2.

questions.question: The number of edges in a regular graph of degree d and n vertices is ____________.

keywords.example: The Petersen graph is 3-regular on 10 vertices: 10*3/2 = 15 edges
keywords.explain: Every vertex has the same degree d

keywords.example: A 4-cycle has 4 vertices of degree 2: sum=8=2*4 edges
keywords.explain: Sum of all vertex degrees = 2|E|, since each edge is counted once from each endpoint

understand.visual_alt: Each of n vertices contributes degree d to the total degree sum nd; the handshaking lemma gives nd = 2|E|, so |E| = nd/2.
understand.plain: In a d-regular graph every vertex has exactly d neighbors. The handshaking lemma says the sum of all vertex degrees equals twice the edge count. With n vertices each of degree d, sum of degrees = nd, so 2|E| = nd, giving |E| = nd/2.

questions.formula_note: The handshaking lemma (sum of degrees = 2|E|) applied to a d-regular graph immediately yields |E| = nd/2; this is the fundamental formula.
questions.to_find: Edge count formula for a d-regular graph on n vertices
